related_chapters.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
related_chapters.why: Like the black folk of Altamaha who followed John proudly to the station to send him off, this chapter recounts a community lifting up one of its own to leave home for college.

related_chapters.subject: Charles F. ("Chuck") McDew
related_chapters.chapter_title: The Drive South and First Segregation
related_chapters.why: As John, schooled in the North, suddenly felt the color line he had not seen before, this chapter describes a student's first direct encounter with segregation on the way to college in the South.

related_chapters.subject: Freddie Greene (Biddle)
related_chapters.part_title: Going Away While the Movement Burned
related_chapters.why: Echoing John's pull between his schooling and the family he left behind in Altamaha, this chapter weighs leaving for college against a brother imprisoned and a home in turmoil.

related_chapters.subject: John Churchville
related_chapters.topic: Opening the School for Ten Dollars a Week
related_chapters.why: Like John returning to Altamaha to open a school for the children of his town, this chapter describes founding and teaching a community school on next to no money.

related_chapters.subject: Judge D'Army Bailey
related_chapters.part_title: Coming Home, and the Lorraine Motel
related_chapters.why: As John came back to Altamaha estranged and changed by his years away, this chapter reflects on returning home to a relationship with one's community altered by all that happened in between.

related_chapters.subject: Clarence Magee
related_chapters.part_title: Legacy and the Work That Remains
related_chapters.chapter_title: A Great-Uncle Lynched, and Restorative Justice
related_chapters.why: Where the story ends with John's lynching by the Judge's mounted men, this chapter confronts a lynching within the speaker's own family and the long reckoning that followed.

top_voice.subject: Esther M. A. Terry
top_voice.reason: Her account of leaving a family farm in rural North Carolina for Bennett College, borne up by her community, sits closest to John's departure from Altamaha as his town pinned its hopes on his coming.
